evidence: tests/support/integration/config.py imports tests.support.integration.plugin and re-exports private internals via module aliases (`_enforce_live_fixture_policy`, `_is_integration_mode`, `_is_truthy_env`), creating direct dependency on another module's non-public API.

tests/support/integration/config.py combines policy wiring, API-client construction, cleanup orchestration, peer-selection helpers, and multiple fixtures in one module, while tests/support/integration/markers.py separately handles marker composition; responsibilities are split across files but tightly coupled through shared internals.

tests/integration/test_smoke.py depends on marker wrappers from tests/support/integration/markers.py while other integration tests depend on fixtures from tests/support/integration/config.py, increasing blast radius when integration policy changes.

src/thenvoi/integrations/claude_sdk/session_manager.py and src/thenvoi/integrations/codex/stdio_client.py each implement bespoke async lifecycle orchestration (background tasks, cancellation, shutdown ordering, pending-operation failure) instead of a shared integration-runtime service pattern.

src/thenvoi/runtime/contacts/formatting.py embeds a large operational system prompt constant (`HUB_ROOM_SYSTEM_PROMPT`) in the same module as event-formatting utilities, mixing behavioral policy content with runtime formatting logic.
examples/prompts/characters.py embeds extensive behavior policy strings directly in code, reinforcing string-defined behavior surfaces across modules rather than centralized prompt/policy boundaries.

Import style drifts inside sibling example entrypoints: examples/a2a_bridge/01_basic_agent.py uses `from setup_logging import setup_logging` while examples/a2a_gateway/01_basic_gateway.py and 02_with_demo_agent.py use package-qualified imports.

examples and docker runners implement near-identical startup choreography with local variation (docker/claude_sdk/runner.py vs examples/claude_sdk_docker/runner.py), creating multiple conventions for the same role.
tests/example_agents/conftest.py mutates sys.path to import examples instead of relying on package/module boundaries.
examples/a2a_gateway/basic_gateway.py catches broad Exception from load_runtime_config and rethrows ValueError, collapsing distinct failure causes into one user-facing error.

examples/langgraph/standalone_sql_agent.py catches broad Exception during DB download and silently switches to fallback DB creation, turning transport/runtime failures into implicit success-path behavior.
src/thenvoi/integrations/parlant/tools.py translates heterogeneous failures into string payloads (`tool_result_type(data=...)`) instead of typed errors, while other modules raise exceptions directly.

Boundary error contracts vary by seam: raised exceptions (examples/a2a_gateway/basic_gateway.py), in-band dict error fields (examples/langgraph/standalone_calculator.py), and tool result text payload errors (src/thenvoi/integrations/parlant/tools.py).
Runner boundary composition is duplicated across docker/claude_sdk/runner.py and examples/claude_sdk_docker/runner.py with divergent mount/prompt behavior, which increases seam drift risk.
The compatibility pass-through docker/shared/config_loader.py adds another seam layer without policy/translation, increasing handoff surface with no behavior normalization.

Adapter entry seams keep both typed turn-context and legacy arg-bag calling paths alive: `legacy_chat_turn_compat`/`legacy_control_turn_compat` in `src/thenvoi/core/simple_adapter.py` and `src/thenvoi/core/control_plane_adapter.py`, plus direct widened `on_message` signature in `src/thenvoi/adapters/codex.py`.

Core tool abstraction is very broad: `AgentToolsProtocol` in `src/thenvoi/core/protocols.py` composes messaging, participants, contacts, memory, schemas, and dispatch, while adapters like `src/thenvoi/adapters/langgraph.py` only need a narrower slice (`MessagingDispatchToolsProtocol`).

Protocol conformance tests (`tests/core/test_protocols.py`) require a dummy tool object implementing the full composite surface, reinforcing coupling to a large abstraction even for scenarios that only need subsets.
Optional dependency loading is explicit and guarded in adapter code paths (`claude_sdk`, `crewai`) rather than implicit import-time crashes.
No clear duplicated-runtime-library conflict is evident in assigned files; framework-specific dependencies map to their adapters.
Network/process calls in script utilities generally include timeouts (`requests.post(..., timeout=30)`, subprocess timeout handling in `docker/shared/repo_init.py`).
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: This batch does not include full lock/dependency manifests, so unresolved duplicate or stale dependency risk cannot be ruled out.

evidence: Multiple files use precise typing and dataclass/Pydantic modeling for configuration surfaces.
Common helper extraction exists for focused concerns (e.g., `system_updates`, `examples/common/bootstrap`).
Despite large files, many local functions retain descriptive names and explicit parameterization.
low_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: Very large adapter files (especially `codex.py`) increase local control-flow and mutation complexity.

evidence: src/thenvoi/integrations/a2a_bridge/session.py returns internal SessionData objects directly from get()/list_sessions(), so boundary behavior depends on external mutation and makes invariant-focused tests harder to write.

src/thenvoi/integrations/a2a_bridge/handler.py explicitly treats `None` as a valid legacy success path in the handler contract, which creates a high-risk path where malformed handler outcomes can pass silently unless tests are very explicit.

src/thenvoi/runtime/types.py validates CALLBACK handlers with inspect.iscoroutinefunction(self.on_event), but the file has no companion guard/normalization path for callable instances with async __call__, leaving a subtle API edge case easy to miss in tests.

holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows `tests/runtime/` has 20 files (>10 mixed-concern threshold) while contacts tests are split between `tests/runtime/` (`test_contact_handler.py`, `test_contact_tools.py`) and `tests/runtime/contacts/`.

`src/thenvoi/runtime/__init__.py` states the runtime root should expose core lifecycle APIs and that domain helpers belong in dedicated submodules, but `src/thenvoi/runtime/contact_tools.py` and `src/thenvoi/runtime/custom_tools.py` still occupy runtime root as compatibility-era modules.
`src/thenvoi/runtime/custom_tools.py` is flagged in holistic context as orphaned (zero importers), indicating stale placement debt remains in a high-visibility package path.
Adapter entrypoints carry dual contracts (typed turn context plus legacy arg-bag compatibility), with normalization logic in base classes and mixed adoption in concrete adapters.

Core runtime classes are oversized and multi-purpose: `ExecutionContext` mixes lifecycle, queueing, hydration, dedupe, retries, and sync recovery; `AgentTools` mixes tool models, schema export, platform API calls, and dispatch behavior.
Tool invocation behavior is duplicated across layers (`normalize_tool_arguments`, `dispatch_platform_tool_call`, and `AgentTools.execute_tool_call`), increasing drift risk and indirection cost.

Near-duplicate runner scaffolding appears in docker/claude_sdk/runner.py and examples/claude_sdk_docker/runner.py (same bootstrap flow, env extraction, prompt/tool loading, adapter construction, and lifecycle orchestration).

Integration tests include repeated narrative banners and step-by-step commentary/logging that restates test code behavior (tests/integration/test_multi_agent.py, tests/integration/test_websocket.py, tests/integration/test_participant_permissions.py).

Multiple adapter/converter test modules use nearly identical long preambles describing where shared tests live, with low incremental signal per file (tests/adapters/test_parlant_adapter.py, tests/adapters/test_pydantic_ai_adapter.py, tests/converters/test_anthropic.py, tests/converters/test_parlant.py, tests/converters/test_pydantic_ai.py).

findings.summary: Integration support has a hub module that centralizes unrelated concerns and amplifies cross-file coupling.
evidence: `tests/support/integration/config.py` contains environment/settings access, cleanup workflows, peer selection, and multiple fixtures in one file.
`tests/support/integration/markers.py` separately defines marker composition, but tests consume both modules together for one integration workflow.
Changes to integration gating or fixture behavior require touching shared support modules used across many tests, increasing ripple risk.
findings.suggestion: Split integration support into explicit boundaries (for example `clients.py`, `cleanup.py`, `selection.py`, `fixtures.py`, `markers.py`) and keep each boundary imported via public APIs only; keep test modules depending on a thin fixture façade rather than internal helpers.

findings.summary: A support module depends on another support module’s private symbols, creating brittle boundary contracts.
evidence: `tests/support/integration/config.py` imports `tests.support.integration.plugin as integration_plugin` and aliases underscored functions (`_enforce_live_fixture_policy`, `_is_integration_mode`, `_is_truthy_env`).
These names are explicitly private by convention, so cross-module reuse can break on internal refactors without clear contract signaling.
findings.suggestion: Expose a small public policy interface from the plugin module (non-underscored functions or a dataclass/service object) and update config/fixture modules to consume only that public surface.

findings.summary: Integration clients reimplement similar lifecycle orchestration patterns instead of sharing one architectural runtime primitive.
evidence: `ClaudeSessionManager` uses command queues plus background task lifecycle (`start`, `stop`, `_run_session_loop`, per-command futures).
`CodexStdioClient` independently manages process creation, dual read loops, cancellation, and pending-request failure logic in `connect`/`close`.
Both modules solve similar ownership/shutdown concerns with different semantics, increasing long-term divergence and maintenance cost.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared async integration lifecycle base (task registry, cancellation policy, close semantics, pending-operation failure policy) and have both integration managers compose/extend it.

findings.summary: Behavioral policy text is embedded directly in runtime/example code, blurring ownership between policy content and execution logic.
evidence: `HUB_ROOM_SYSTEM_PROMPT` in `formatting.py` is a large operational policy block colocated with event formatting functions.
`examples/prompts/characters.py` encodes large character-policy templates in module-level string constants.
Prompt/policy updates require code edits in logic modules rather than updating dedicated policy assets or a prompt registry.
findings.suggestion: Move prompt/policy bodies into a dedicated prompt-policy package or data assets with explicit versioned loading, and keep runtime modules focused on formatting/execution concerns.

findings.summary: Example entrypoints mix package imports, bare local imports, and sys.path mutation.
evidence: examples/a2a_bridge/01_basic_agent.py imports `setup_logging` via `from setup_logging import setup_logging` instead of package-qualified import.
examples/a2a_gateway/01_basic_gateway.py imports `main` from `examples.a2a_gateway.basic_gateway` using package-qualified style.
tests/example_agents/conftest.py injects example directories into sys.path to make imports work.
findings.suggestion: Standardize example import conventions around package-qualified imports and run examples as modules (`python -m ...`/`uv run -m ...`); remove sys.path mutation by making examples importable through a stable package boundary.

findings.summary: Runner startup logic is duplicated with divergent conventions across docker and examples.
evidence: docker/claude_sdk/runner.py and examples/claude_sdk_docker/runner.py both orchestrate bootstrap_runner, prompt composition, adapter creation, and run lifecycle with mostly parallel code paths.
The two files diverge on required mounts and prompt composition inputs (repo init/context only in docker variant), despite serving the same runner role.
docker/shared/config_loader.py is a pure pass-through alias to thenvoi.testing.config_loader.load_runner_config, adding another convention surface without behavior.
findings.suggestion: Extract a single shared runner composition function/module and keep environment-specific differences as explicit config data (mounts, repo-init flag, prompt extras), then collapse pass-through wrappers that add no policy.

findings.summary: Integration boundaries mix exception propagation, in-band error payloads, and silent fallback.
evidence: examples/a2a_gateway/basic_gateway.py catches Exception and re-raises ValueError for runtime loading failures.
examples/langgraph/standalone_sql_agent.py catches Exception from download and silently creates an unrelated fallback DB schema.
src/thenvoi/integrations/parlant/tools.py converts failures to `tool_result_type(data=...)` text errors rather than raising typed failures.
examples/langgraph/standalone_calculator.py reports errors via `{"result": None, "error": ...}` return shape instead of exception or typed result object.
findings.suggestion: Define one integration error contract (typed exception hierarchy or typed result object) and apply it uniformly at seams; only convert to human-readable strings at final UI/output boundaries.

findings.summary: Module handoffs rely on ad-hoc seam contracts instead of one explicit boundary protocol.
evidence: docker/shared/config_loader.py is a pass-through seam with no translation/policy, while runners still own orchestration and validation details.
docker/claude_sdk/runner.py and examples/claude_sdk_docker/runner.py duplicate seam choreography with subtle differences, indicating missing shared boundary abstraction.
src/thenvoi/integrations/parlant/tools.py builds a separate ad-hoc error/result transport protocol (`tool_result_type(data=...)`) that is not shared by neighboring integrations.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared seam contract for runner/tool integrations (common bootstrap spec + typed boundary result/error model), then refactor entrypoints to compose that contract rather than re-implementing handoffs per module.

findings.summary: Legacy arg-bag compatibility keeps adapter boundary contracts wider than needed.
evidence: `SimpleAdapter` and `ControlPlaneAdapter` normalize legacy arg-bag calls into turn-context objects, but `CodexAdapter.on_message` still publicly exposes the full legacy argument bag in its signature and manually normalizes.
Assigned tests actively call adapters with legacy-style parameter bags (`msg/tools/history/...`) rather than a single typed turn context, showing the wide seam is still in active use.
findings.suggestion: Introduce one canonical `on_message(turn=...)` contract for new code and confine arg-bag compatibility to thin adapters generated by a shared compatibility layer; migrate tests to typed turn contexts first, then deprecate direct arg-bag signatures (starting with `CodexAdapter`).

findings.summary: Composite tool protocol is too broad for many adapter use cases.
evidence: `AgentToolsProtocol` combines chat, contacts, memory, schema export, and dispatch into one large surface.

Multiple adapters type against `AgentToolsProtocol` even when they primarily need message/event + tool dispatch behavior, while `LangGraphAdapter` demonstrates a narrower protocol (`MessagingDispatchToolsProtocol`) is viable.
Protocol runtime-check tests enforce full-surface implementations, increasing coupling and mock burden.
findings.suggestion: Split adapter dependencies to minimal capability protocols per integration path (for example: dispatch+messaging, contact-aware, memory-aware) and migrate adapters/tests to those slices; keep `AgentToolsProtocol` as a convenience aggregate, not the default dependency type.

findings.summary: Optional integration dependencies use conflicting import/failure strategies.
evidence: `crewai.py` imports optional packages in module scope and raises on import failure.
`langgraph.py` imports framework types at module import time without a deferred availability guard.
`claude_sdk.py` captures ImportError and defers hard failure through `_ensure_claude_sdk_available()`, creating a different operational contract from sibling adapters.
findings.suggestion: Standardize optional dependency policy across adapters: either uniformly defer failures to adapter construction/start with explicit guard helpers, or uniformly fail at import with documented behavior; implement one shared helper and apply it across all optional integrations.

findings.summary: Tool-dispatch seam conflates failures with successful string payloads.
evidence: `AgentTools.execute_tool_call()` returns error text strings for validation and dispatch failures instead of a typed failure result or raised exception.
`CodexAdapter._handle_server_request()` treats any returned string as normal output and sends `success: true`; only exception paths produce `success: false`.
Current tests verify exception-driven validation failure handling, but not the returned-error-string path, leaving this contract mismatch exposed.
findings.suggestion: Replace string-sentinel failures with a typed result (for example `ToolExecutionResult{ok,error,payload}`) or raise explicit exceptions from `execute_tool_call`; update adapter bridge code to map failures to `success: false` consistently and add tests for unknown-tool/dispatch-failure paths.

findings.summary: Session store leaks mutable internal state across module boundaries.
evidence: InMemorySessionStore.get() returns the stored SessionData object directly (`return self._sessions.get(room_id)`), and list_sessions() returns direct references (`list(self._sessions.values())`).
SessionData is a mutable Pydantic model; callers can mutate `last_activity` without going through store APIs, changing eviction behavior externally.
Runtime boundary types in runtime/types.py are also mutable dataclasses, reinforcing a pattern of unguarded cross-module state mutation.
findings.suggestion: Make boundary DTOs immutable (or return defensive copies) and provide explicit store methods for lifecycle updates (for example, `touch_session(room_id)`), so session mutation always flows through lock-protected store logic.

findings.summary: Bridge handler contract mixes implicit and explicit outcome semantics.
evidence: BaseHandler.handle in handler.py returns `HandlerResult | None`, with docstring saying `None` means implicit success.
HandlerResult introduces explicit statuses (`handled`, `ignored`, `error`), but the same seam still permits unstructured completion via `None`.
Nearby runtime seam (`MessageHandler` in runtime/types.py) uses a different contract style (`Awaitable[None]`), increasing integration ambiguity between related handler abstractions.
findings.suggestion: Unify handler seams on one explicit contract: require structured outcome objects for bridge handlers (deprecate implicit `None`), and align runtime handler type aliases/docs so boundary behavior is uniformly predictable.

findings.summary: Current boundary design creates silent-failure paths that need targeted contract tests.
evidence: handler.py intentionally allows implicit success via `None`, so accidental handler return-shape regressions can look successful unless tests explicitly assert contract normalization behavior.
session.py exposes live session objects, so behavior can be altered externally without store API calls, making tests vulnerable to hidden coupling and stateful side effects.
Both files represent critical routing/session paths, but their contracts rely on implicit behavior rather than fail-fast boundaries, increasing the chance of regressions escaping unit tests.
findings.suggestion: Add strict contract tests around these seams: enforce explicit handler outcomes (or explicit normalization warnings/errors), and add tests that verify session state cannot be mutated externally without going through store methods.

findings.summary: Runtime root mixes core API surface with legacy compatibility modules, weakening folder ownership boundaries.
evidence: `src/thenvoi/runtime/__init__.py` documents a core-only runtime surface and directs domain helpers to dedicated submodules.
`src/thenvoi/runtime/contact_tools.py` is explicitly a deprecated compatibility shim pointing elsewhere, yet remains at runtime root.
`src/thenvoi/runtime/custom_tools.py` remains in runtime root and is marked orphaned in holistic context, indicating lingering legacy placement.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) Target folder: consolidate legacy shims under `src/thenvoi/runtime/compat/` (or `src/thenvoi/compat/runtime/`) and keep runtime root focused on core runtime contracts only. (2) Move order: first add new compat modules and wire shim registry; second update imports in code/tests from `thenvoi.runtime.contact_tools`/`thenvoi.runtime.custom_tools` to canonical modules (`thenvoi.runtime.contacts.contact_tools` and the chosen custom-tools home); third deprecate and remove root-level stragglers. (3) Import-update/validation commands: `rg -n "thenvoi\.runtime\.(contact_tools|custom_tools)" src tests`, then run `uv run pytest tests/runtime/test_contact_handler.py tests/runtime/test_contact_tools.py tests/runtime/test_custom_tools.py -v` and `uv run pytest tests/test_agent.py -v`.

findings.summary: Contacts-related tests are split between a flat runtime test directory and a dedicated contacts subpackage, reducing navigability and ownership clarity.
evidence: Assigned contacts tests live in `tests/runtime/` even though a dedicated `tests/runtime/contacts/` package exists.
holistic_context.structure reports `tests/runtime/` has 20 files (flat overload signal) while contacts-specific tests also exist in `tests/runtime/contacts/`.
Both assigned files import contacts-domain symbols (`thenvoi.runtime.contacts.*`), indicating affinity with the contacts test package.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) Target folder: move `tests/runtime/test_contact_handler.py` and `tests/runtime/test_contact_tools.py` into `tests/runtime/contacts/` with names aligned to existing package conventions. (2) Move order: move one file at a time, keep temporary import compatibility where needed, then normalize local fixture imports in the moved tests. (3) Import-update/validation commands: `rg -n "tests\.runtime\.test_contact_(handler|tools)|runtime/test_contact_(handler|tools)" tests`, run `uv run pytest tests/runtime/contacts/ -k "contact_handler or contact_tools" -v`, then `uv run pytest tests/runtime/ -v`.

findings.summary: The runtime package’s declared public contract is not consistently reinforced by nearby tests, leaving the intended architectural boundary implicit.
evidence: `src/thenvoi/runtime/__init__.py` defines a curated runtime surface via lazy exports.
`tests/test_agent.py` primarily imports concrete internals (`thenvoi.runtime.types`) rather than asserting boundary behavior through `thenvoi.runtime` surface.
`tests/conftest.py` wires broad shared plugins, but no package-boundary contract test is present among assigned files for runtime root exports/placement promises.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) Target folders: keep contract tests in `tests/runtime/` and domain tests in `tests/runtime/contacts/`; add a dedicated runtime-surface contract test file under `tests/runtime/` to lock intended import boundary. (2) Move order: first add runtime-surface assertions (`thenvoi.runtime` exports and deprecation import paths), second relocate contacts behavior tests to contacts subfolder, third remove redundant direct-internal import assertions where boundary tests replace them. (3) Import-update/validation commands: `rg -n "from thenvoi\.runtime(\.| import)" tests`, then `uv run pytest tests/runtime/test_namespace.py tests/runtime/test_tools_direct_module.py tests/test_agent.py -v`.

findings.summary: Integration config depends on private plugin symbols, creating fragile cross-module contracts.
evidence: `tests/support/integration/config.py` binds `_enforce_live_fixture_policy`, `_is_integration_mode`, and `_is_truthy_env` from `tests.support.integration.plugin` using private-name imports.
Private-name reach-in bypasses explicit module contracts and increases break risk when plugin internals change.
Marker and fixture policy logic are split across multiple modules, increasing boundary ambiguity.
findings.suggestion: Promote a public policy API in `tests/support/integration/plugin.py` (for example `integration_policy.py` exports), update `config.py` and `markers.py` to consume only public functions/constants, and add a small contract test asserting policy API behavior and import stability.

findings.summary: README governance promises are not encoded in repository-local default execution paths.
evidence: `README.md` says contract checks are required on every PR and points to `uv run pytest -m contract_gate -v`.
`pyproject.toml` default pytest options are `-m 'not integration'` and do not include `contract_gate`, so default local test runs can miss declared required contracts.
Policy intent exists in docs and marker definitions, but execution defaults do not force/compose the policy.
findings.suggestion: Make governance executable: add a dedicated test alias/script (for example `test:quality-gates`) in project tooling, include `contract_gate` in that canonical command, and reference exactly one authoritative command in README and CI so docs/runtime policy cannot drift.

findings.summary: Integration test admission policy is duplicated between markers and fixture-time skips.
evidence: Marker decorators (`requires_api`, `requires_multi_agent`, `requires_user_api`) encode env requirements.
Fixtures in `config.py` independently call `pytest.skip(...)` when env vars are absent, allowing tests without required markers to still pass as skipped.
This creates two policy layers with different enforcement points, weakening detection of mis-marked tests.
findings.suggestion: Centralize admission in one layer: enforce marker presence for live fixtures (hard-fail on missing marker in integration mode), keep env-check logic in a shared policy function, and reserve fixture skips only for explicitly marked integration tests.

findings.summary: High-fan-in reusable modules are located in `examples/`, obscuring ownership boundaries.
evidence: `examples/common/bootstrap.py` and `examples/prompts/characters.py` are among top-imported files (16 and 13 importers respectively), used across multiple framework examples and tests.
Holistic directory profiles classify `examples/prompts/` as script zone while these files behave like shared contracts.
Current placement implies demo scope even though repo governance treats them as contract-check targets.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) create `src/thenvoi/example_support/bootstrap.py` and `src/thenvoi/example_support/prompts/characters.py`; 2) move logic there while leaving thin compatibility shims in old `examples/` paths; 3) update imports in framework examples and tests to new package paths; 4) remove shims after one release cycle. Validate with `uv run ruff check . --fix`, `uv run pyrefly check`, `uv run pytest tests/ --ignore=tests/integration/ -v`, and `uv run pytest -m contract_gate -v`.

findings.summary: Adapter API is split between legacy arg-bag and typed turn-context contracts.
evidence: `SimpleAdapter.normalize_chat_turn()` and `ControlPlaneAdapter.normalize_control_turn()` both accept either context objects or legacy parameter bags.
Most adapters use `@legacy_chat_turn_compat` with context-only signatures, but `CodexAdapter.on_message()` still directly exposes union/optional legacy parameters and performs manual normalization.
This keeps two call styles alive simultaneously and leaks compatibility mechanics into business handlers.
findings.suggestion: Define one canonical adapter entrypoint (`on_message(turn_context)`), move legacy compatibility into a temporary boundary wrapper outside adapter implementations, and enforce the canonical signature across all adapters.

findings.summary: Runtime orchestration and tool execution are concentrated into oversized multi-role classes.
evidence: `ExecutionContext` (978 LOC) owns room state, queue/process loop, participant tracking, dedupe cache, retry strategy, sync reconciliation, and shutdown/idle signaling in one class.
`AgentTools` (1159 LOC) contains Pydantic input models, tool schema rendering for providers, business operations, and dispatch/error translation in one implementation.
Protocols are split into granular interfaces, but concrete runtime wiring collapses them back into broad single classes, reducing leverage of those boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime into focused components (e.g., message loop coordinator, participant state service, history hydration service, tool schema provider, tool dispatcher), then compose them behind thin facades.

findings.summary: Tool argument/default logic is duplicated across bridge and runtime dispatch layers.
evidence: `tool_bridge.py` has both `normalize_tool_arguments()` and `dispatch_platform_tool_call()` with repeated per-tool defaulting/shape logic.
`AgentTools.execute_tool_call()` performs its own validation/normalization path before delegating to bridge dispatch, creating overlapping responsibility.
There is already visible duplication noise (`thenvoi_send_event` sets `metadata` default twice in `normalize_tool_arguments()`), indicating drift risk from parallel logic tables.
findings.suggestion: Create one authoritative tool contract registry (arguments, defaults, dispatcher binding, error formatting) and have `AgentTools`, bridge dispatch, and binding factories consume that registry instead of re-encoding behavior.

findings.summary: Error event reporting pattern is copy-pasted across adapters instead of centralized.
evidence: Multiple adapters implement nearly identical `_report_error()` logic (`tools.send_event('Error: ...', 'error')` + broad exception fallback).
LangGraph repeats the same best-effort error-event block inline inside `on_message()` instead of using a shared helper.
This duplicates failure semantics and increases chance of subtle divergence in nonfatal handling.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared adapter error-reporting utility/mixin that standardizes error event payloads and nonfatal recording, then remove per-adapter bespoke copies.

findings.summary: Runner entrypoints reimplement the same bootstrap/prompt/agent lifecycle skeleton.
evidence: Each runner builds similar flow: bootstrap config, resolve role/prompt, instantiate adapter, create runner agent, log startup, run lifecycle.
Small variant differences are mixed into full copies of the same lifecycle logic, especially between Claude runner variants.
`examples/run_agent.py` carries another parallel runner-spec/kwargs composition path rather than reusing one runner composition abstraction.
findings.suggestion: Move to a single declarative runner template API in `runner_core` where adapter-specific options are provided as data/hooks, not copied `main()` flows.

findings.summary: Large integration test files mix provisioning, orchestration, and assertion concerns.
evidence: `test_codex_adapter.py` concentrates fake transport implementation, schema helpers, setup utilities, and a very large behavior matrix in one module.
`test_participant_permissions.py` combines dynamic resource lifecycle management, multiple fixtures, and two broad matrix suites in a single file.
`test_multi_agent.py` and `test_history_converters.py` include heavy scenario setup plus assertions together, increasing cognitive load and reducing focused test intent.
findings.suggestion: Split scenario families into focused modules, move reusable provisioning/fake clients into shared test support packages, and isolate matrix generation from assertion logic.

findings.summary: Claude SDK runner flow is copy-pasted across production and example runners with only small parameter differences.
evidence: Both runner files perform the same sequence: bootstrap config, derive model/prompt/tools/workspace/role, instantiate ClaudeSDKAdapter, create runner agent, log startup fields, and call run_agent_lifecycle().
Differences are mostly mount list and optional repo_init wiring, indicating duplication of orchestration template rather than distinct domain logic.
A compatibility wrapper (docker/shared/config_loader.py) also re-exports thin config-loading behavior, reinforcing shallow layering around duplicated runner scaffolding.
findings.suggestion: Extract one shared runner-template builder in thenvoi.testing.runner_core (or a dedicated runner module) that accepts a small dataclass of overrides (mounts, extra prompt sections, repo init hook), and keep docker/example entrypoints as minimal declarative specs.

findings.summary: Several integration tests contain excessive narration and decorative logging that duplicates executable assertions.
evidence: Tests repeatedly emit separator banners and procedural logs ("Testing:", repeated agent identity logs, scenario narration) before/after assertions.
Large block comments and docstrings restate what the test method body already encodes step-by-step.
Signal-to-noise is reduced in failures because many log lines are descriptive prose rather than unique diagnostic context.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a lean integration-test logging convention: keep only failure-relevant IDs/state snapshots, remove banner prose, and move scenario explanations to short class/module docstrings or parametrized test names.

findings.summary: Adapter/converter tests repeat long boilerplate preambles that provide little per-file specificity.
evidence: Each file starts with a similarly structured paragraph explaining that shared behavior lives elsewhere and this file contains framework-specific checks.
The repeated template text is long relative to unique content and changes rarely, creating synchronization drift risk across files.
The pattern suggests generated boilerplate propagation instead of a single canonical test documentation location.
findings.suggestion: Replace repeated per-file preambles with a short one-liner and centralize shared testing policy in one helper module or docs page referenced from each file.

findings.summary: Sibling example entrypoints follow inconsistent logging/bootstrap/import conventions, creating style islands.
evidence: `examples/run_agent.py` and coding scripts configure logging directly with `logging.basicConfig`, while many examples use shared logging profiles.
`examples/codex/basic_agent.py` imports adapter classes from `thenvoi.adapters.codex`, while peer examples use `from thenvoi.adapters import ...`.
Bootstrap flow is inconsistent across example families (shared `examples.common.bootstrap` in some, bespoke setup in others).
findings.suggestion: Define and enforce one example-entrypoint contract: standardized logging helper, standardized adapter import path policy, and a shared bootstrap utility for credentials/platform URLs. Add a lightweight lint/test that validates example modules against this contract.

findings.summary: Equivalent runtime failures are handled with incompatible strategies (raise, return, stringify), making caller behavior unpredictable.
evidence: `ParlantAdapter.on_message` returns after logging when app is uninitialized instead of raising.
`LangGraphAdapter.on_message` emits an error event then re-raises exceptions.
`CrewAIToolRuntime.execute_tool` catches exceptions and returns JSON error payloads as normal string results.
`generate_token.py` mixes `SystemExit` and `ValueError` for related failure classes.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared error policy matrix by boundary (adapter internal, tool execution, script CLI) and implement common helpers: invariant failures should raise typed exceptions, user-visible failures should be emitted once with preserved context, and CLI scripts should normalize exit behavior through one exception-to-exit mapper.

findings.summary: Abstractions are uneven: oversized adapter classes coexist with low-value forwarding wrappers.
evidence: `src/thenvoi/adapters/codex.py` (1837 LOC) aggregates transport lifecycle, approval protocol, model fallback, tool dispatch, and event emission.
`docker/shared/config_loader.py` is a pass-through adapter to `thenvoi.testing.config_loader.load_runner_config` with no policy translation.
`crewai_tooling.py` and `pydantic_ai.py` both build dynamic tool wrappers with overlapping concerns instead of sharing a bounded abstraction.
findings.suggestion: Split `CodexAdapter` into focused collaborators (client lifecycle, approval manager, turn/event mapper, model policy). Remove/pass-inline no-policy wrappers like `docker/shared/config_loader.py`. Extract a common adapter-tool binding layer consumed by CrewAI and PydanticAI adapters.

findings.summary: Example scenarios are manually replicated across frameworks with minimal semantic differences.
evidence: Each framework keeps separate basic/custom/Tom/Jerry scripts with highly similar orchestration and only adapter-level substitutions.
Prompt and roleplay behaviors are centralized, but surrounding script scaffolding remains duplicated.
This duplication pattern is repeated for multiple scenario families, increasing synchronized-edit burden.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a scenario registry (scenario definition + framework adapter factory) that generates or dispatches framework variants from shared scenario definitions. Keep framework-specific deltas in small adapter hooks rather than full script copies.

findings.summary: Directory layout shows objective overload in examples and adapters, reducing navigability and ownership clarity.
evidence: `examples/langgraph/` has `file_count: 12` (all script zone) and mixes tutorial examples with standalone app-like utilities.
`examples/` directory profile contains mixed `production` and `script` zones, signaling blurred placement policy.
`src/thenvoi/adapters/` has `file_count: 12`, `total_loc: 5322`, with one dominant file (`codex.py`) acting as a structural hotspot.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1 target folders: move standalone utilities into `examples/langgraph/standalone/` and numbered walkthroughs into `examples/langgraph/scenarios/`; move cross-framework scenario prompts into `examples/scenarios/prompts/`. Stage 2 move order: (1) create new folders and move passive modules (`prompts.py`, standalone files), (2) move numbered examples, (3) update `examples/run_agent.py` registry paths. Stage 3 adapter packaging: split `src/thenvoi/adapters/codex.py` into `src/thenvoi/adapters/codex/{adapter.py,approval.py,turns.py,events.py}` and keep `__init__.py` as facade. Stage 4 import and validation commands: `uv run ruff check . --fix`, `uv run ruff format .`, `uv run pyrefly check`, `uv run pytest tests/adapters/test_codex_adapter.py tests/example_agents/ -v`.

findings.summary: A shared adapter lifecycle exists conceptually but is implemented with diverging per-framework policies and duplicated orchestration code.
evidence: All adapters perform similar steps (bootstrap metadata/history, inject system updates, invoke framework/tool loop, report/send errors), but each file hand-rolls these seams differently.
Shared helper scope is narrow (`system_updates` only), while lifecycle/error/reporting composition is duplicated across adapters.
Behavioral divergence appears in invariant checks and failure propagation style, indicating pattern drift rather than intentional specialization only.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a stricter adapter skeleton in core (template method hooks for history staging, metadata injection, tool reporting, and error propagation). Keep framework-specific calls in narrow hook methods and enforce conformance with shared adapter contract tests.
